steps.title: Identify the research finding about age and borrowing
steps.content: The passage gives us a key finding: younger speakers of nonmajority languages borrow more from the majority language than older speakers do.

steps.title: Connect the research to the choices
steps.content:
Choice D (‘younger speakers of Spanish in New York City would be more likely to use a dialect specific to New York City than older speakers living in the same neighborhood would be.’) states that younger Spanish speakers would be more likely to use the NYC-specific dialect than older speakers.

This follows directly from the research: younger speakers borrow more from the majority language (English), and the hypothesis claims the NYC dialect develops through English interaction.

The other choices either rely on unsupported neighborhood logic, predict general language growth or contradict the research by focusing on borrowing from other nonmajority languages.

That confirms D is correct.
